name: MLIR
summary: Lattner et al.'s 2020 generalization of multi-level IR: every IR is a self-contained dialect (operations, types, verification rules); dialects coexist and lower into each other. Substrate for Mojo, IREE, Triton, Torch-MLIR, Polygeist, ClangIR, and many other modern compiler stacks.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
MLIR (Lattner et al., 2020) generalizes the multi-level IR pattern: instead of a fixed cascade, every IR is a *dialect* — a self-contained set of operations, types, and verification rules — and dialects can coexist in one MLIR module. Lowering between dialects is a transformation pass (the `linalg` dialect lowers to `scf` lowers to `llvm`); analyses and optimizations can target any dialect that exposes their structure.

The contribution: making "the IR you want" cheap to define. Adding a new dialect for a domain (CUDA `gpu`, Linalg structured-loop, SPV for SPIR-V, Tensor) is a few hundred lines; integrating it with the rest of MLIR is automatic.

sections.title: Core concepts
sections.content:
MLIR organizes IR around dialects, operations, types, attributes, and progressive lowering. Each dialect declares its operations and verification rules; the framework provides shared infrastructure (rewrite-pattern engine, type interfaces, conversion framework, region-based control flow, block parameters as the SSA merge form).

This is how Mojo, IREE, Triton, Torch-MLIR, Polygeist, and many other projects build their compilers — they don't write a compiler from scratch; they write a few dialects on top of MLIR.

sections.title: Multi-level lowering
sections.content:
A major strength of MLIR is that it encourages compilers to lower through multiple semantically meaningful stages instead of flattening everything into one universal representation immediately. A typical flow goes high-level domain dialect → progressively lower dialects → `llvm` dialect → LLVM IR → machine code.

This lets domain-specific reasoning run on the right abstraction at the right time: a tensor compiler can do shape inference and operator fusion in `linalg` before lowering to scalar loops, and only commit to LLVM IR when target-specific codegen begins.

sections.title: ClangIR — C/C++ as an MLIR dialect
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): ClangIR (CIR) — an MLIR-based high-level IR for C and C++ — is the most consequential MLIR dialect added to the LLVM mainline since 2020. The ClangIR incubator was frozen for new development on 2026-02-20 with development moved fully into the LLVM monorepo; CIR is invoked via `clang -fclangir`.

CIR sits between Clang's internal AST and LLVM IR, exposing C/C++ language constructs (constructors, exceptions, virtual dispatch, RAII, atomics) as MLIR operations rather than the immediate translation to LLVM IR that Clang historically performed. The motivation: Clang's traditional AST → LLVM IR lowering loses language-level structure that high-level analyses (ownership inference, lifetime analysis, exception-flow tracking) want. CIR is the canonical fix: a structured, MLIR-based IR that retains C/C++ semantics through a chunk of the optimization pipeline before lowering to LLVM IR.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
MLIR's flexibility is powerful, but it brings framework complexity: dialect design burden, conversion-pattern boilerplate, and the discipline of choosing which abstraction belongs at which level. Not every compiler needs this degree of infrastructure — a smaller backend-oriented stack (Cranelift, QBE) is often a better fit when only one IR layer is wanted.

The right question is whether the workload genuinely needs multiple semantically meaningful IR layers (tensor compilers, GPU kernel compilers, hardware-synthesis compilers — yes; small AOT compilers for one source language — usually no).
